claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

The first thing I need to know is about the child (or children) you're claiming for:

**How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please let me know their ages and we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi there! Yes, I'd like to claim for my three children. 

I have Alex who's 5, Blake who's 17, and Charlie who's 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! Let me assess each of your three children:

- **Alex** (age 5) - Under 16
- **Blake** (age 17) - 16 to 19
- **Charlie** (age 19) - 16 to 19

Let me start by checking the requirements for each child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex (age 5)**, since he's under 16, he automatically qualifies for the child age requirement. However, I need to continue through a few more checks.

**Is Alex (age 5) working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?**

(Given Alex is only 5, I'm assuming the answer is yes - please confirm!)

Also, for **Blake (17)** and **Charlie (19)**, since they're both in the 16-19 age range, I'll need to ask:

**Are Blake and Charlie currently in one of the following?**
- Approved full-time education (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3)
- Approved training (such as an unpaid traineeship in England or Employability Fund programme in Scotland)

Please let me know the situation for each of them!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, that's right - Alex is only 5, so he's not working at all!

As for Blake and Charlie - Blake left school and isn't in any education or training at the moment. 

But Charlie is different - he's still studying, doing his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! Let me work through each child's situation:

---

**Alex (age 5)** ✓ - Not working, so that's fine. Let me continue his assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex (age 5)**: Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Again, given Alex is only 5, I'd expect the answer is no - please confirm!)

---

**Blake (age 17)** - Since Blake has left school and isn't in any education or training, I need to check something:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For Blake, since he's not in education or training, there may still be an extension period that could apply. 

**When did Blake leave school/education?** Was it recent (within the last few months), or has it been a while?

---

**Charlie (age 19)** - Great news that Charlie is doing A-levels! That's approved education. I'll need to verify a few more details about Charlie's situation as well.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
That's right, Alex doesn't receive any benefits - he's only 5!

As for Blake, he left school a few months back. He hasn't really figured out what he wants to do next yet.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Let me continue the assessments:

---

**Alex (age 5)** - Perfect, let me continue his checks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex (age 5)**: Is Alex in an apprenticeship? (I'm assuming no, given he's only 5!)

---

**Blake (age 17)** - Since Blake left school "a few months back," there might be an extension period that could still apply. Let me check the rules:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is important for **Blake**! There's a **20-week extension period** that may apply. To qualify, Blake needs to:

1. Have left education within the last **20 weeks**
2. Be registered with a qualifying service such as:
   - A government careers service
   - Connexions
   - Armed services

**Two questions about Blake:**
1. Has it been less than 20 weeks since he left school?
2. Has Blake registered with any careers service or similar support service?

---

**Charlie (age 19)** - I'll also need to verify a few things about Charlie's education to make sure it qualifies. But first, let me confirm about Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, Alex definitely isn't in an apprenticeship - he's only 5!

As for Blake, yes it's been less than 20 weeks - probably around 3 months or so since he left. And actually, yes, he did sign up with the local careers service recently to try and figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! That's very helpful. Let me continue the assessments:

---

**Alex (age 5)** - Great, let me move forward with his assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about **your** situation (the claimant). This applies to all three children, so I only need to ask once:

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status AND are working/seeking work/studying/have sufficient resources
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad

**Do you normally live in the UK?** (This is the most common situation)

---

**Blake (age 17)** - Great news! Since:
- It's been less than 20 weeks since he left school (around 3 months ≈ 12-13 weeks)
- He's registered with the local careers service

Blake should qualify for the **20-week extension period**! This means Child Benefit can continue for him while he figures out his next steps. The extension will end either when the 20 weeks are up or when he turns 18, whichever comes first.

---

**Charlie (age 19)** - I still need to verify a few things about Charlie's A-level studies. But let's confirm your residency first, as it applies to all children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Living in the UK satisfies the residency requirement for all three children. Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for the children. **Are you responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

This means either:
1. **You live with them**, OR
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)

Do all three children live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That confirms your responsibility for all three children. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of your three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?**

For example, this could be another parent, a grandparent, or someone else. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. Now I need to check about the children's living situations.

**Are any of the children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently in any of the following situations?**

1. **In hospital** for an extended period
2. **In local authority care**
3. **In a fostering arrangement**

Or are they all living at home with you under normal circumstances?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're all living at home with me under normal circumstances. None of them are in hospital, care, or fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards the accommodation or maintenance of any of your children?**

In other words, are you fully supporting Alex, Blake, and Charlie without financial assistance from the local council for their accommodation or upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - I'm supporting all of them myself. The council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment for Alex and Blake. Now I just need to verify a couple of details about Charlie's education to confirm his eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Charlie (age 19)** who is studying A-levels at sixth form college, I just need to confirm a couple of things:

1. **Is Charlie studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)

2. **Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week** (or not working at all)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, Charlie is studying full-time at sixth form - it's definitely more than 12 hours a week of classes and supervised study.

And no, Charlie doesn't work at all - he's focused on his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great news! A-levels are approved (non-advanced) education, Charlie is studying full-time, and he's not working. I just need to confirm a couple more things about Charlie specifically:

1. **Is Charlie receiving any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

2. **Is Charlie married or in a civil partnership?** (If yes, is he financially dependent on you?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, Charlie isn't receiving any benefits in his own right.

And no, he's not married or in a civil partnership - he's just living at home with me while he finishes his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's all I need to know. Based on all the information you've provided, I can now give you the eligibility outcome for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News! You are eligible for Child Benefit for all three children!

Here's a summary of your eligibility:

### ✅ Alex (age 5) - **ELIGIBLE**
Alex qualifies because he is under 16 years old. As long as he continues living with you and the other conditions remain the same, you can receive Child Benefit for him until he turns 16 (and potentially beyond if he stays in approved education).

---

### ✅ Blake (age 17) - **ELIGIBLE** (with conditions)
Blake qualifies under the **20-week extension period** because:
- He left school less than 20 weeks ago
- He's registered with a careers service

⚠️ **Important:** Blake's eligibility is temporary. It will end when:
- The 20-week extension period expires, OR
- Blake turns 18, whichever comes first

If Blake starts approved education or training before the extension ends, he could continue to be eligible.

---

### ✅ Charlie (age 19) - **ELIGIBLE**
Charlie qualifies because he's in **approved full-time education** (A-levels at sixth form college), studying more than 12 hours per week, and not working.

⚠️ **Important:** Charlie's eligibility will continue until he either:
- Finishes his A-levels, OR
- Turns 20, whichever comes first

---

## Next Steps

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** at [www.gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)
2. You'll need National Insurance numbers and birth certificates for the children

## Additional Information

💷 **High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through a tax charge. You can still claim, but it's worth checking if this applies to you.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, which means he automatically qualifies for the child age requirement. (2) He is not working. (3) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in an apprenticeship. (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex is living at home under normal circumstances. (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 17) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Although Blake is 16-19 and has left education, he qualifies for the 20-week extension period. (2) He left school less than 20 weeks ago (approximately 3 months/12-13 weeks). (3) He has registered with the local careers service, which is a qualifying service for the extension. (4) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). (5) The claimant is responsible for Blake as he lives with them. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. (7) Blake is living at home under normal circumstances. (8) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Note: The extension will end either when 20 weeks have passed since leaving education OR when Blake turns 18, whichever comes first.

Charlie (age 19) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved (non-advanced) education - A-levels at sixth form college. (2) He is studying full-time (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study). (3) He is not working (well under the 24 hours per week limit). (4) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. (5) He is not married or in a civil partnership. (6) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). (7) The claimant is responsible for Charlie as he lives with them. (8) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. (9) Charlie is living at home under normal circumstances. (10) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Note: Eligibility will continue until Charlie finishes his A-levels or turns 20, whichever comes first.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children. The claimant meets all the general requirements: they live in the UK (Manchester), all three children live with them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for these children, and the local council is not contributing to the children's accommodation or maintenance. Alex qualifies as he is under 16. Blake qualifies under the 20-week extension period as he left school recently and has registered with a careers service. Charlie qualifies as he is in approved full-time education (A-levels). The claimant should be aware that Blake's eligibility is temporary (until the 20-week extension ends or he turns 18), and Charlie's eligibility will continue until he finishes his A-levels or turns 20.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, which means he automatically qualifies for the child age requirement. (2) He is not working. (3) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in an apprenticeship. (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex is living at home under normal circumstances. (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 17) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Although Blake is 16-19 and has left education, he qualifies for the 20-week extension period. (2) He left school less than 20 weeks ago (approximately 3 months/12-13 weeks). (3) He has registered with the local careers service, which is a qualifying service for the extension. (4) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). (5) The claimant is responsible for Blake as he lives with them. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. (7) Blake is living at home under normal circumstances. (8) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Note: The extension will end either when 20 weeks have passed since leaving education OR when Blake turns 18, whichever comes first.

Charlie (age 19) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved (non-advanced) education - A-levels at sixth form college. (2) He is studying full-time (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study). (3) He is not working (well under the 24 hours per week limit). (4) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. (5) He is not married or in a civil partnership. (6) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). (7) The claimant is responsible for Charlie as he lives with them. (8) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. (9) Charlie is living at home under normal circumstances. (10) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Note: Eligibility will continue until Charlie finishes his A-levels or turns 20, whichever comes first.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children. The claimant meets all the general requirements: they live in the UK (Manchester), all three children live with them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for these children, and the local council is not contributing to the children's accommodation or maintenance. Alex qualifies as he is under 16. Blake qualifies under the 20-week extension period as he left school recently and has registered with a careers service. Charlie qualifies as he is in approved full-time education (A-levels). The claimant should be aware that Blake's eligibility is temporary (until the 20-week extension ends or he turns 18), and Charlie's eligibility will continue until he finishes his A-levels or turns 20.
